era_context: The early 18th century is dominated by the resolution of two great European wars — the War of the Spanish Succession (1701–1714) and the Great Northern War (1700–1721) — which together redrew the political map of Europe. In Western Europe, 1714 marks the definitive end of the Spanish Succession conflict, with France and Spain under Bourbon dynasties and Britain emerging as a major naval and colonial power. In the Baltic, Russia under Peter the Great is displacing Sweden as the dominant power. In Asia, the Qing dynasty under the ageing Kangxi Emperor governs the world's most populous state, while the Mughal Empire fragments after Aurangzeb's death in 1707. The Ottoman Empire under Ahmed III is entering its 'Tulip Era' of cultural flourishing.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa beyond coastal trading posts and the Ethiopian highlands
Interior Southeast Asia (Lan Xang, Burmese kingdoms, Cambodian internal affairs)
Interior South America beyond Iberian colonial settlements
Central Asian khanates beyond Dzungar-Qing frontier interactions

events.title: Treaty of Rastatt ends War of the Spanish Succession between France and the Empire
events.region: Holy Roman Empire / France (Rastatt, modern Germany)
events.description: Signed on 6 March 1714 between Louis XIV of France and Emperor Charles VI of the Holy Roman Empire, the Treaty of Rastatt complemented the 1713 Treaty of Utrecht by ending hostilities between France and the Empire. France ceded the Spanish Netherlands, Naples, Sardinia, and Milan to the Habsburgs. France retained Strasbourg and its Alsatian territories. The treaty confirmed the Bourbon succession in Spain while compensating the Austrian Habsburgs with substantial Italian and Low Countries territories, establishing a new European balance of power that would persist for decades.
key_figures: Louis XIV (King of France)
Charles VI (Holy Roman Emperor)
Prince Eugene of Savoy (Imperial negotiator)
Claude Louis Hector de Villars (French negotiator and Marshal of France)

sources.name: Treaty text, published in contemporary collections of European treaties (e.g., Dumont, Corps universel diplomatique)
French and Imperial diplomatic correspondence from the negotiations
John B. Wolf, Louis XIV (1968); historiographic synthesis of the peace settlement

events.certainty_note: Treaty text survives; date and terms precisely documented by both signatories.

events.title: Death of Queen Anne and Hanoverian succession in Britain
events.description: Queen Anne, the last Stuart monarch, died on 1 August 1714 at Kensington Palace. Under the Act of Settlement 1701, the crown passed not to the Catholic Stuart pretender James Francis Edward Stuart ('the Old Pretender') but to the Protestant Elector of Hanover, who became George I. This dynastic transition was the most consequential political event in British history that year, ending the Stuart line on the throne and inaugurating the Hanoverian dynasty that would rule until 1901. The succession was contested by Jacobites, leading to the 1715 rising, but proceeded peacefully in its immediate execution thanks to Whig preparations and the Privy Council's swift action.
key_figures: Queen Anne (last Stuart monarch, d. 1 August 1714)
George I (Elector of Hanover, King of Great Britain from 1 August 1714)
Robert Harley, Earl of Oxford (Lord Treasurer, dismissed shortly before Anne's death)
Henry St John, Viscount Bolingbroke (Secretary of State, suspected Jacobite sympathiser)
James Francis Edward Stuart ('the Old Pretender')

sources.name: Privy Council records and official proclamation of George I's accession
Jonathan Swift, correspondence and memoirs (contemporary observer)
Edward Gregg, Queen Anne (1980; 2001 revised edition)
Ragnhild Hatton, George I: Elector and King (1978)

events.certainty_note: One of the best-documented political transitions in British history; precise dating from official records.

events.title: Fall of Barcelona ends Catalan resistance in the War of the Spanish Succession
events.description: On 11 September 1714, after a fourteen-month siege, Bourbon forces under the Duke of Berwick stormed Barcelona, the last major holdout of the Grand Alliance cause in Spain. Catalan defenders under Rafael Casanova fought desperately but were overwhelmed. The fall of Barcelona ended Catalan self-governance: Philip V subsequently issued the Nueva Planta decrees, abolishing Catalonia's historic institutions, laws, and privileges and imposing Castilian administrative structures. The date — 11 September — is now commemorated as the Diada, Catalonia's national day, and remains a potent symbol in Catalan nationalism.
key_figures: James FitzJames, Duke of Berwick (Bourbon commander)
Rafael Casanova (Chief Councillor of Barcelona, wounded in the final defence)
Antonio de Villarroel (military commander of Barcelona's defence)
Philip V (Bourbon King of Spain)

sources.name: Contemporary siege accounts and Bourbon military records
Catalan municipal records (Dietari del Consell de Cent)
Henry Kamen, The War of Succession in Spain, 1700–15 (1969)
John H. Elliott, Scots and Catalans: Union and Disunion (2018)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented by both besiegers and defenders; precise dating from military and municipal records.

events.title: Battle of Gangut — Russia's first major naval victory
events.region: Baltic Sea / Gulf of Finland (Hanko Peninsula, modern Finland)
events.description: On 27 July (O.S.) / 7 August 1714, a Russian galley fleet commanded by Peter the Great defeated a Swedish naval squadron under Rear Admiral Nils Ehrenskiöld at the Battle of Gangut (Hanko) in the Gulf of Finland. The Russians captured the Swedish flagship and several vessels. This was the first significant Russian naval victory in history and demonstrated that Peter's shipbuilding programme had created a force capable of challenging Swedish dominance in the Baltic. The victory enabled Russian operations against the Swedish-held Finnish coast and the Åland Islands, fundamentally shifting the strategic balance in the Great Northern War.
key_figures: Peter I ('the Great', Tsar of Russia, personally commanded)
Fyodor Apraksin (Russian Admiral, commanded the galley fleet)
Nils Ehrenskiöld (Swedish Rear Admiral, captured)
Charles XII (King of Sweden, absent — in Ottoman exile until 1714)

sources.name: Russian Admiralty records and Peter I's correspondence
Swedish naval records and court-martial proceedings after the defeat
Robert K. Massie, Peter the Great: His Life and World (1980)

events.certainty_note: Documented by both Russian and Swedish naval records; precise Old Style and New Style dates established.

events.title: Treaty of Baden formally concludes the War of the Spanish Succession
events.description: Signed on 7 September 1714, the Treaty of Baden between France and the Holy Roman Empire confirmed and extended the terms of the Treaty of Rastatt. It formally restored peace between France and the individual princes of the Empire, returning territories on the Upper Rhine to their pre-war status. While largely reiterating Rastatt's provisions, the treaty was legally necessary because Rastatt had been concluded between the Emperor and France alone, without the formal consent of the Imperial Diet. Baden thus marked the definitive legal end of the War of the Spanish Succession in Central Europe.
key_figures: Prince Eugene of Savoy (Imperial plenipotentiary)
Claude Louis Hector de Villars (French plenipotentiary)

sources.name: Treaty text, published in Dumont's Corps universel diplomatique and other contemporary collections
Imperial Diet (Reichstag) records at Regensburg

events.certainty_note: Treaty text and ratification records survive; precise date established.

events.title: Charles XII returns to Swedish territory after Ottoman exile
events.description: After over five years of exile in Ottoman territory following his defeat at Poltava in 1709, Charles XII of Sweden made a dramatic journey across Europe, arriving at Stralsund in Swedish Pomerania on 11 November 1714. He had left Demotika (Didymoteicho) in the Ottoman Empire and rode across Habsburg and German territories in disguise. His return reinvigorated Swedish resistance in the Great Northern War but could not reverse the strategic losses already suffered. Charles immediately began reorganising Swedish defences, but Stralsund itself would fall to coalition forces in 1715.
key_figures: Charles XII (King of Sweden)
Ahmed III (Ottoman Sultan, whose hospitality Charles had exhausted)

sources.name: Voltaire, Histoire de Charles XII (1731), based on interviews with participants
Swedish chancery records and contemporary correspondence
Robert I. Frost, The Northern Wars (2000)

events.certainty_note: Date of arrival at Stralsund well-established in Swedish records; details of the journey partly reconstructed from later accounts.

events.title: Schism Act restricts Dissenter education in Britain
events.description: Passed by the Tory-dominated Parliament and receiving royal assent on 25 June 1714, the Schism Act prohibited anyone from keeping a school or acting as a tutor unless they held a licence from the diocesan bishop and conformed to the Church of England. The act was a direct attack on Dissenting (Nonconformist Protestant) academies, which had become important centres of education and intellectual life. However, Queen Anne died on the very day the act was due to take effect (1 August 1714), and the incoming Whig government under George I effectively let it lapse. It was formally repealed in 1719.
key_figures: Henry St John, Viscount Bolingbroke (chief political sponsor)
Queen Anne (gave royal assent)

sources.name: Parliamentary records (House of Lords and Commons Journals, 1714)
Geoffrey Holmes, British Politics in the Age of Anne (1967; revised 1987)

events.certainty_note: Parliamentary records provide exact dates of passage and royal assent.

events.title: Daniel Gabriel Fahrenheit develops the mercury-in-glass thermometer
events.description: In 1714, the Polish-born Dutch-German instrument maker Daniel Gabriel Fahrenheit developed a reliable mercury-in-glass thermometer, a significant improvement over earlier alcohol-based instruments. Mercury's higher boiling point and more uniform expansion allowed much greater accuracy and range. Fahrenheit subsequently developed the temperature scale that bears his name, with fixed reference points. This innovation was critical for the advancement of experimental physics, chemistry, and medicine, providing for the first time a reproducible, quantitative measure of temperature that could be compared across laboratories.
key_figures: Daniel Gabriel Fahrenheit (instrument maker and physicist)

sources.name: Fahrenheit's own communications to the Royal Society (published in Philosophical Transactions, 1724, describing work from c. 1714)
W. E. Knowles Middleton, A History of the Thermometer and Its Uses in Meteorology (1966)

events.certainty_note: The exact year of the mercury thermometer's development is conventionally given as 1714 based on Fahrenheit's later accounts, but the precise dating is approximate; his work evolved over several years (c. 1709–1724).

events.title: Kangxi Emperor's compilation of the Kangxi Dictionary nears completion
events.description: The Kangxi Dictionary (Kangxi Zidian), commissioned by the Kangxi Emperor in 1710, was in its final stages of compilation by 1714 and completed in 1716. This monumental Chinese dictionary contained approximately 47,035 characters and became the standard reference work for Chinese characters for over two centuries. Its compilation involved dozens of scholars and represented both an intellectual achievement and a political act — demonstrating Manchu patronage of Chinese literary culture and the Qing dynasty's claim to cultural legitimacy as rulers of China.
key_figures: Kangxi Emperor (Aisin Gioro Xuanye, Qing Emperor, r. 1661–1722)
Zhang Yushu (chief editor, d. 1711)
Chen Tingjing (chief editor, d. 1712)

sources.name: Kangxi Emperor's imperial edicts commissioning and overseeing the project
Jonathan Spence, Emperor of China: Self-Portrait of K'ang-hsi (1974)

events.certainty_note: The project's timeline is documented in Qing court records; the dictionary's completion is dated to 1716, with 1714 falling within the final compilation phase.

events.title: Tuscarora War concludes in colonial North Carolina
events.description: The Tuscarora War (1711–1715) reached its effective conclusion in March 1714 with a peace treaty between the colonial government of North Carolina and the remaining Tuscarora bands under Chief Tom Blount. The war had begun as a Tuscarora uprising against colonial encroachment and abuses by traders. After devastating defeats — particularly the fall of Fort Neoheroka in March 1713 — most Tuscarora were killed, enslaved, or displaced. Surviving groups migrated northward to join the Iroquois Confederacy as a sixth nation. The war opened vast tracts of eastern North Carolina to colonial settlement.
key_figures: Chief Tom Blount (Tuscarora leader who negotiated peace)
James Moore Jr. (South Carolina militia commander who led the 1713 campaign)

sources.name: Colonial Records of North Carolina (edited by William L. Saunders, 1886)
Christoph von Graffenried's account (contemporary settler who was captured by the Tuscarora)
David La Vere, The Tuscarora War: Indians, Settlers, and the Fight for the Carolina Colonies (2013)

events.certainty_note: Colonial records document the peace negotiations; some specific dates within 1714 are less precise than the overall timeline.

events.title: Philip V of Spain issues Nueva Planta decree for Catalonia
events.description: Following the fall of Barcelona in September, Philip V began implementing the Decretos de Nueva Planta for the former Crown of Aragon territories. The decree for Catalonia, issued on 16 January 1716 but rooted in decisions taken immediately after the 1714 conquest, abolished the Catalan Corts (parliament), the Generalitat, the Consell de Cent, Catalan civil law in many areas, and the use of Catalan in official administration. These decrees imposed Castilian legal, administrative, and linguistic structures, effectively ending Catalonia's autonomous political existence within the Spanish monarchy — a status it had held since the medieval period.
key_figures: Philip V (King of Spain)
Jean Orry (French adviser who helped design the new administrative system)

sources.name: Decretos de Nueva Planta, royal decrees (text published in Novísima Recopilación)
Henry Kamen, Philip V of Spain: The King Who Reigned Twice (2001)

events.certainty_note: The formal decree for Catalonia is dated January 1716, but the policy decisions and initial implementation followed directly from the September 1714 conquest. This event is included here as the political consequence of the fall of Barcelona.

events.title: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz publishes Monadology
events.description: In 1714, the German philosopher and mathematician Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz composed the Monadologie (Monadology) while in Vienna, one of his most influential philosophical works. Written in French for Prince Eugene of Savoy, the treatise set out Leibniz's mature metaphysical system: that reality consists of simple, indivisible substances called 'monads,' each reflecting the entire universe from its own perspective. Though not published in full during his lifetime (it circulated in manuscript and appeared posthumously in 1720), 1714 is the established date of composition. The work profoundly influenced subsequent rationalist philosophy, particularly Christian Wolff and later Kant.
key_figures: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz (philosopher, mathematician, d. 1716)
Prince Eugene of Savoy (dedicatee)

sources.name: Leibniz's manuscript of Monadologie (1714), preserved in the Leibniz archives, Hanover
Maria Rosa Antognazza, Leibniz: An Intellectual Biography (2009)

events.certainty_note: Manuscript is dated to 1714 from internal evidence and Leibniz's correspondence; first publication was posthumous (1720).

events.title: Ottoman Empire enters the Tulip Era under Ahmed III
events.description: By 1714, the Ottoman Empire under Sultan Ahmed III and his Grand Vizier Nevşehirli Damat İbrahim Pasha (appointed 1718, but the cultural trajectory was underway) was entering what historians later termed the 'Tulip Era' (Lâle Devri, c. 1718–1730). While the formal dating is typically 1718–1730, the cultural and diplomatic opening that characterised this period — including Ottoman interest in European printing technology, garden culture, diplomatic engagement, and architectural patronage — had roots in the years following the Treaty of Karlowitz (1699). In 1714, Ottoman attention was partly directed toward the Venetian-held Morea (Peloponnese), which the Ottomans would reconquer in 1715.
key_figures: Ahmed III (Ottoman Sultan, r. 1703–1730)

sources.name: Ottoman court chronicles (Silahdar Findiklili Mehmed Ağa, Nusretname)
Can Erimtan, Ottomans Looking West? The Origins of the Tulip Age and Its Development in Modern Turkey (2008)

events.certainty_note: The broader Tulip Era is a historiographic construct whose exact boundaries are debated; events within Ahmed III's reign are well-documented by Ottoman chroniclers.

events.title: Johor Sultanate succession crisis in the Malay world
events.description: The assassination of Sultan Abdul Jalil IV of Johor in 1699 had set off a protracted succession crisis that continued to roil the Malay political world in 1714. The Johor Sultanate, once the dominant Malay polity and successor to the Malacca Sultanate, was fragmenting under pressure from the Bugis diaspora, internal Malay rivalries, and Dutch and other European commercial interests. By the 1710s, real power in Johor was increasingly contested by Malay and Bugis factions, foreshadowing the rise of Bugis influence that would dominate the sultanate by mid-century. The era illustrates the complex interplay of indigenous and colonial power in maritime Southeast Asia.
key_figures: Abdul Jalil Riayat Shah IV (Sultan of Johor, r. 1699–1720, installed after the assassination of his predecessor)

sources.name: Tuhfat al-Nafis (Precious Gift), Bugis-Malay chronicle compiled by Raja Ali Haji in the 19th century
Dutch VOC records from Malacca factory
Leonard Y. Andaya, The Kingdom of Johor 1641–1728 (1975)

events.certainty_note: VOC records provide contemporary documentation; Malay chronicle sources are later compilations with uncertain chronology for specific years.

events.title: George I arrives in Britain and forms Whig government
events.description: George I, the new Hanoverian king, landed at Greenwich on 18 September 1714, over six weeks after Queen Anne's death. He immediately began dismantling the Tory ministry, dismissing Bolingbroke and installing a Whig-dominated government. This inaugurated a period of Whig political supremacy that would last nearly five decades. Key appointments included Charles Townshend as Secretary of State and James Stanhope in a senior role. The political transition was swift and decisive: Bolingbroke fled to France and joined the Jacobite court, and Robert Harley was later impeached and imprisoned in the Tower of London.
key_figures: George I (King of Great Britain)
Charles Townshend, 2nd Viscount Townshend (Secretary of State)
James Stanhope (Secretary of State for the Southern Department)
Henry St John, Viscount Bolingbroke (fled to France)

sources.name: Calendar of State Papers, Domestic Series, George I
Ragnhild Hatton, George I: Elector and King (1978)

events.certainty_note: Official records precisely document George I's arrival and the ministerial changes.

events.title: Mughal Emperor Farrukhsiyar's reign and political instability
events.description: Farrukhsiyar, who had seized the Mughal throne in 1713 with the support of the Sayyid Brothers (Husain Ali Khan and Abdullah Khan), continued his troubled reign in 1714. Real power was exercised by the Sayyid Brothers, who held the positions of Mir Bakshi and Wazir respectively. The emperor's attempts to assert independent authority created constant factional tension at the Mughal court. This period exemplified the rapid decline of central Mughal authority following Aurangzeb's death in 1707, as provincial governors and court factions accumulated independent power at the expense of the throne.
key_figures: Farrukhsiyar (Mughal Emperor, r. 1713–1719)
Husain Ali Khan (Mir Bakshi, one of the Sayyid Brothers)
Abdullah Khan (Wazir, one of the Sayyid Brothers)

sources.name: Khafi Khan, Muntakhab al-Lubab (contemporary Mughal chronicle)
William Irvine, Later Mughals (1922, reprinted)

events.certainty_note: Contemporary Mughal chronicles and European factory records document Farrukhsiyar's reign in detail.

events.title: Spanish Royal Academy (Real Academia Española) founded
events.description: The Real Academia Española was formally established in 1713 and received royal approval from Philip V on 3 October 1714. Modelled on the Académie française, the institution was charged with cultivating and standardising the Castilian (Spanish) language. Its founding motto — 'Limpia, fija y da esplendor' ('It cleans, fixes, and gives splendour') — reflected the Enlightenment-era impulse toward linguistic codification. The Academy's first major project was the Diccionario de autoridades (1726–1739), which became the foundational reference for Spanish lexicography. The RAE remains the principal authority on the Spanish language.
key_figures: Juan Manuel Fernández Pacheco, Marquis of Villena (first director)
Philip V (King of Spain, granted royal charter)

sources.name: Royal charter of the Real Academia Española (3 October 1714)
Alonso Zamora Vicente, Historia de la Real Academia Española (1999)

events.certainty_note: Founding charter survives with precise date; institutional records are comprehensive.

events.title: Tokugawa Ietsugu rules as child shogun in Japan
events.description: In 1714, Japan was nominally governed by Tokugawa Ietsugu, the seventh Tokugawa shogun, who had assumed the position in 1713 at the age of three. Real administrative power was exercised by senior councillors (rōjū) and the shogunal advisers. The year 1714 is also notable in Japanese cultural history for the Ejima-Ikushima affair, a scandal involving a lady of the shogunal inner court (Ejima) and a kabuki actor (Ikushima Shingorō), which led to severe punishments and tightened regulations on kabuki theatre and the inner chambers of Edo Castle. The affair revealed tensions between the austere Confucian governance ideals promoted by the recently deceased adviser Arai Hakuseki and the actual culture of the shogunal court.
key_figures: Tokugawa Ietsugu (seventh Tokugawa shogun, r. 1713–1716, child ruler)
Ejima (lady of the ōoku / inner chambers)
Ikushima Shingorō (kabuki actor)

sources.name: Tokugawa jikki (True Records of the Tokugawa), official shogunal chronicle
Cecilia Segawa Seigle, Yoshiwara: The Glittering World of the Japanese Courtesan (1993) and related scholarship on the Ejima-Ikushima affair

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in official Tokugawa records; the Ejima-Ikushima affair generated extensive contemporary documentation.

events.title: Ethiopian Emperor Dawit III's brief reign amid Gondarine instability
events.description: The Ethiopian Empire based at Gondar experienced continued political instability in the early 18th century. Emperor Yostos had been deposed in 1711, and the throne passed through several short-reigning monarchs. Dawit III (r. 1716–1721) would soon take the throne in this turbulent period. The Gondarine era (1632–1769) was characterised by the growing power of regional Oromo lords and the increasing marginalization of the Solomonic emperors, foreshadowing the Zemene Mesafint ('Era of the Princes') when emperors became mere figureheads. The period is documented primarily through the royal chronicles of Gondar, which provide court-centric but detailed accounts.
key_figures: Yostos (Emperor, deposed 1711)
Various Gondarine court officials and regional lords

sources.name: Royal Chronicles of Gondar (edited and translated by various scholars including Ignazio Guidi)
Donald Crummey, Land and Society in the Christian Kingdom of Ethiopia (2000)

events.certainty_note: Gondarine chronicles provide narrative but exact dating of some events within specific years can be uncertain; the overall political trajectory is well-established.

events.title: Leibniz composes the Principes de la Nature et de la Grâce
events.description: Also in 1714, Leibniz composed a second major philosophical treatise, the Principes de la Nature et de la Grâce fondés en Raison (Principles of Nature and Grace Founded in Reason), written for Prince Eugene of Savoy alongside the Monadology. This shorter work offered a more accessible presentation of his metaphysical system and included his famous question: 'Why is there something rather than nothing?' — which became one of the central questions of Western metaphysics. The two works together represent the culmination of Leibniz's philosophical system, composed just two years before his death in 1716.
key_figures: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz (philosopher and mathematician)

sources.name: Leibniz manuscript, Principes de la Nature et de la Grâce (1714)
Maria Rosa Antognazza, Leibniz: An Intellectual Biography (2009)

events.certainty_note: Manuscript dated to 1714 from Leibniz's correspondence and internal evidence.

events.title: Maratha expansion continues under Peshwa Balaji Vishwanath
events.description: By 1714, the Maratha Confederacy under Shahu (grandson of Shivaji) was consolidating its power in the Deccan, with Balaji Vishwanath serving as Peshwa (chief minister) from 1713. Balaji Vishwanath was the founder of the Peshwa dynasty that would come to dominate the Maratha state. In this period, the Marathas were exploiting the factional chaos of the Mughal court to extend their influence, collecting chauth (tribute) across large swathes of the Deccan and gradually challenging the Mughal governor (Nizam) of the region. The Maratha expansion represented the most significant political-military force in the Indian subcontinent during the post-Aurangzeb era.
key_figures: Shahu I (Chhatrapati of the Maratha Empire)
Balaji Vishwanath (Peshwa, r. 1713–1720)

sources.name: Marathi bakhars (contemporary and near-contemporary Maratha narrative histories)
Stewart Gordon, The Marathas 1600–1818 (New Cambridge History of India, 1993)

events.certainty_note: Maratha administrative records (Modi script documents) and bakhar literature provide detailed chronology for this period.

events.title: Siege of Mallorca — last Bourbon conquest in the War of the Spanish Succession
events.description: Following the fall of Barcelona in September, Bourbon forces turned to Mallorca (Majorca), the last territory still holding out for the Archduke Charles's cause in the War of the Spanish Succession. The island, which had declared for Charles III (the Habsburg claimant) in 1706, was blockaded and invaded in June-July 1715, but its resistance was rooted in the political alignments established by 1714. Like Catalonia, Mallorca subsequently lost its autonomous institutions under the Nueva Planta decrees. The Balearic Islands' involvement illustrates how the War of the Spanish Succession was also a civil war within the Spanish domains.
key_figures: Marquis of Lede (Bourbon military commander for the Balearic campaign)

sources.name: Bourbon military campaign records
Henry Kamen, The War of Succession in Spain, 1700–15 (1969)

events.certainty_note: The actual military invasion of Mallorca occurred in 1715; included here as the political context was established by the 1714 mainland resolution.

disconfirming_evidence: Several points of complexity or dispute should be noted: (1) The dating of Fahrenheit's mercury thermometer to 1714 is conventional but imprecise — his thermometric work evolved over roughly 1709–1724, and some historians place the key innovation earlier or later. (2) The 'Tulip Era' as a distinct Ottoman period is a historiographic construct, primarily coined by Ahmed Refik Altınay in the early 20th century; some modern scholars (e.g., Can Erimtan) argue the concept retrojects modern Turkish nationalist narratives onto the Ottoman past and distorts the period's actual character. (3) The fall of Barcelona on 11 September 1714 is today deeply politicised in Catalan nationalism; while the military events are well-documented, the interpretation of the siege as a 'national liberation struggle' is contested by historians who note that the war was a dynastic succession dispute rather than a proto-nationalist movement. (4) The smoothness of the Hanoverian succession is sometimes overstated — there is evidence of significant Jacobite planning and even some ministerial ambivalence (Bolingbroke's contacts with the Pretender) that could have led to a different outcome had Anne lived longer or died at a more politically volatile moment. (5) Leibniz's Monadology and Principles of Nature and Grace are sometimes conflated or confused in popular accounts; they are distinct texts written for different audiences in the same year.
historiographic_note: 1714 is an exceptionally well-documented year in European history, owing to the convergence of major diplomatic settlements, dynastic transitions, and military campaigns. Sources are overwhelmingly European and court-centric: treaty texts, parliamentary records, diplomatic correspondence, and official chronicles. Non-European documentation for this year is also substantial for the Mughal Empire (Persian-language chronicles, European factory records), Qing China (court records, gazetteers), and Tokugawa Japan (bakufu records). However, coverage of sub-Saharan Africa, interior Southeast Asia, Oceania, and indigenous American societies remains thin, dependent on European colonial records that capture only those indigenous events that intersected with European interests. The surviving sources for all regions carry significant biases: European sources privilege elite political and military history; Mughal and Qing chronicles are court-sponsored; and accounts of defeated parties (Catalans, Tuscarora, Jacobites) are often filtered through victors' narratives or reconstructed from later sources.

graph_edges.note: Treaty of Rastatt complements the Treaty of Utrecht as part of the wider Spanish Succession peace settlement
The Hanoverian succession was mandated by the Act of Settlement 1701
The Hanoverian succession provoked the 1715 Jacobite rising
The fall of Barcelona enabled the abolition of Catalan institutions via the Nueva Planta decrees
Russian naval expansion in the Baltic was enabled by the Swedish military collapse after Poltava
Russian Baltic naval supremacy contributed to Sweden's eventual capitulation at Nystad
Charles XII's Ottoman exile was a direct consequence of his defeat at Poltava
Charles's return to active command culminated in his death at Fredriksten in 1718
Defeated Tuscarora migrated north and were adopted as the sixth nation of the Iroquois Confederacy
Mughal political fragmentation under Farrukhsiyar was a direct consequence of the succession crises following Aurangzeb's death
Maratha expansion exploited the power vacuum created by Aurangzeb's death and subsequent Mughal disintegration
Ottoman strategic confidence under Ahmed III led to the reconquest of the Morea from Venice in 1715
Both events represent the final resolution of the War of the Spanish Succession in different theatres
